section.title: Grow in Grace
section.sectionRationale: Peter closes the letter by turning the certainty of the coming Day into a call to live. Because a new creation is promised, believers are to make every effort to be found at peace, spotless and blameless. The Lord's patience is to be counted as salvation, a truth Paul also wrote, though his letters, like the rest of Scripture, are twisted by the unstable to their ruin. So the readers, forewarned, must guard against being carried away by error and instead grow in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ, to whom all glory belongs.
section.thesis: In view of the coming Day and the promised new creation, believers are to pursue peace and blamelessness, to read the Lord's patience as salvation, to guard against the error that distorts Scripture to its own destruction, and above all to keep growing in the grace and knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ, to whom be the glory forever.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the call to be found at peace, spotless and blameless, verse 14; patience as salvation and the twisting of Scripture, verses 15 to 16; and the closing charge to stand firm and grow in grace, with a doxology, verses 17 to 18.

verses.text: Therefore, beloved, as you anticipate these things, make every effort to be found at peace, {{aspilos|spotless}} and {{amometos|blameless}} in His sight.

units.label: Patience is salvation
units.note: Paul also wrote, and Scripture is twisted

verses.text: Consider also that our Lord’s {{makrothumia|patience}} brings salvation, just as our beloved brother Paul also wrote you with the wisdom God gave him.

He writes this way in all his letters, speaking in them about such matters. Some parts of his letters are hard to understand, which ignorant and unstable people {{streblousin|distort}}, as they do the rest of the Scriptures, to their own destruction.

units.label: Stand firm and grow
units.note: grace, knowledge, and glory to Christ

verses.text: Therefore, beloved, since you already know these things, be on your guard so that you will not be carried away by the error of the lawless and fall from your {{sterigmos|secure standing}}.
But {{auxanete|grow}} in the {{charis|grace}} and {{gnosis|knowledge}} of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. To Him be the glory both now and to the day of eternity. Amen.

aspilos.gloss: Without spot, unstained, unblemished. Believers are to be found spotless when Christ appears, a deliberate contrast to the false teachers who were called blots and blemishes. The word describes moral purity kept clean from the corruption of the world.

amometos.gloss: Blameless, without fault, beyond reproach. Paired with spotless, it pictures the believer standing before God with nothing justly charged against him. It is sacrificial language, the wholeness once required of an offering, now the character in which Christ's people are to be found.

makrothumia.gloss: Patience, long-suffering, forbearance. The very patience that the scoffers mistook for slowness is here named salvation: the Lord's delay is the time in which sinners are saved. What mockers despise, believers are to count as mercy and opportunity.

streblousin.gloss: To twist, wrench, or distort, as one racks or strains something out of shape. Ignorant and unstable people wrench Paul's harder words, and all Scripture, into meanings that suit them, and so destroy themselves. The verb pictures the violence done to the text by those without stability in the truth.

sterigmos.gloss: Firmness, steadfastness, a secure and settled standing. Believers are warned not to be carried away and lose their stability, the very quality the unstable lack. It is the firm footing in the truth from which error would drag them away.

auxanete.gloss: To grow, increase, become greater. The letter's closing command is not merely to hold ground but to grow, a present continuous call to ongoing increase. The safeguard against being carried away is not stagnation but living growth in Christ.

charis.gloss: Grace, God's unearned favor and the power by which the Christian life is lived. Believers are to grow in grace, sinking deeper into the favor of God in Christ. It is the same grace the false teachers twisted into license, here held out as the soil of true maturity.

gnosis.gloss: Knowledge, here a growing personal knowledge of our Lord and Savior Jesus Christ. The whole letter has pressed the knowledge of Christ as the ground of godliness; it now ends by commanding its increase. To grow in this knowledge is the true antidote to the errors that destroy.

basic.context: Peter draws the letter to a close. Because the Day is coming and a new creation is promised, believers should make every effort to be found at peace with God, spotless and blameless, the opposite of the false teachers who were stains and blemishes. They should regard the Lord's patient delay as salvation, the very thing Paul also taught in his letters. Peter adds a striking note: Paul's letters contain things hard to understand, which unstable people twist, just as they twist the rest of Scripture, to their own ruin. So the readers, already warned, must guard against being swept away by the error of the lawless and losing their firm footing. Instead they are to keep growing in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ, to whom Peter gives glory now and forever.
basic.hermeneutics: This is the letter's conclusion, gathering its themes into final exhortation. Read verse 14 in light of chapter 3's certainty of the Day: hope produces holiness. Verses 15 to 16 are important for what they say about Scripture, placing Paul's letters alongside the rest of the Scriptures, and warning that difficult texts can be distorted by the unstable, a warning about the interpreter's heart as much as the text's difficulty. Verse 18's command to grow is present tense, an ongoing pursuit, and the closing doxology to Christ carries its own weight as worship offered to the Lord Jesus.
basic.originalLanguages: Believers are to be found aspilos, spotless, and amometos, blameless, counting the Lord's makrothumia, patience, as salvation. Unstable people streblousin, twist, Paul's words and all Scripture to their ruin. So the readers must keep their sterigmos, firm standing, and auxanete, keep growing, in the charis, grace, and gnosis, knowledge, of Jesus Christ, to whom be glory forever.
basic.typology: The language of spotless and blameless draws on the Old Testament sacrifice, which had to be without defect. What was required of the offering becomes the character in which Christ's people are to be found, fulfilled in Christ the spotless Lamb and worked out in those who are His.
basic.christ: The letter ends where it has always pointed: in Christ. He is the Lord in whose sight believers are to be found spotless and blameless, the One whose patience is salvation, and the object of the grace and knowledge in which His people grow. The closing doxology, glory both now and to the day of eternity, is offered directly to Him, a clear confession of the deity of Jesus. To grow in the knowledge of Christ is the whole aim of the Christian life and the sure guard against every error.
basic.god: God is gracious and patient, and His patience is salvation for those who repent. He is the God who gave Paul wisdom to write Scripture, so that the apostolic letters carry the authority of the rest of the Scriptures. He is the God whose grace is the soil of Christian growth, and to Christ the Lord all glory belongs, now and forever. The God who guarantees the coming Day is the same God who keeps and matures His people until it.
basic.crossReferences: Ephesians 1:4 speaks of being holy and blameless before Him, the goal of verse 14. Romans 2:4 shows God's patience leading to repentance, behind verse 15. 2 Timothy 3:16 affirms that all Scripture is God-breathed, alongside Peter's placing of Paul's letters among the Scriptures. And Ephesians 4:15 calls believers to grow up into Christ, the aim of verse 18.
basic.oneStory: Scripture ends its warnings not in fear but in growth. The whole story of God's people moves toward a Day of reckoning and a new creation, and the fitting response is to be found in Christ, spotless and steadily maturing. Against the unstable who twist the word to their ruin stand those who grow in grace and knowledge, kept by the God who gave the Scriptures and glorified in the Son to whom the letter gives its final praise.

context.literary: These verses form the letter's conclusion, marked by the repeated beloved and by therefore, drawing conclusions from all that precedes. The structure moves from exhortation to holiness, verse 14, to a supporting appeal to Paul and a warning about twisting Scripture, verses 15 to 16, to a final charge and doxology, verses 17 to 18. The letter ends, as it began, on the knowledge of Christ.
context.historical: Peter writes as the false teachers are actively unsettling the churches. His reference to Paul shows the early recognition of Paul's letters as authoritative and widely circulated, and his complaint that they are twisted reflects a real controversy over their meaning, likely including Paul's teaching on grace and freedom that the libertines abused. The unstable are those without grounding, easily swept along by the confident lawless.
context.canonical: Verses 15 to 16 are a landmark text: an apostle places Paul's letters alongside the rest of the Scriptures, an early witness to the emerging New Testament canon and to the inspired authority of the apostolic writings. The passage also warns of the danger of private, self-serving interpretation, a theme that runs through the church's whole approach to reading Scripture rightly.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the letter to a close by turning eschatological certainty into ethical urgency: to be found spotless and at peace, to read God's patience as saving mercy, to beware the distortion of Scripture by the unstable, and to press on in the grace and knowledge of Christ, ending in worship of Him.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: This section is overwhelmingly prescriptive: make every effort, be on your guard, grow. Verse 16 also describes a danger, the twisting of Scripture, but it too functions as warning. The abiding commands are to pursue blamelessness, to hold a firm standing, and above all to keep growing in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ.

debates.question: What does it mean that unstable people twist Paul's letters, and how does verse 16 bear on Scripture, verses 15 to 16?

views.label: It affirms Paul's letters as Scripture and warns against distorting hard texts
views.case: Peter places Paul's letters among the rest of the Scriptures, an early and weighty recognition of their inspired authority. The problem is not the difficulty of some passages in itself but the ignorant and unstable readers who wrench them, and all Scripture, to fit their own ends, to their own destruction. The safeguard is a settled grounding in the truth and humble submission to the text.

views.label: It merely notes that Paul is sometimes obscure
views.case: A lighter reading takes the verse simply as Peter's candid admission that Paul can be hard to follow, without a strong claim about canon or a warning about the reader's heart. On this view the accent falls on Paul's difficulty rather than on the distorters. Yet Peter's own stress is on the unstable who twist, and on the ruin that follows, which points beyond mere obscurity.

debates.bearing: The stronger reading, that Peter affirms Paul's writings as Scripture and warns against self-serving distortion, has shaped the church's doctrine of canon and its care in interpretation. Either way the pastoral point holds: hard texts are not a license for reckless handling, and stability in the truth guards against the twisting that destroys.

debates.question: Why does the letter end with a command to grow rather than merely to hold fast, verse 18?

views.label: Growth in grace and knowledge is the true safeguard against error
views.case: Peter has just warned against being carried away and losing one's firm standing. The remedy he gives is not static defense but living growth: keep increasing in the grace and knowledge of Christ. A maturing believer, deepening in Christ, is far less prey to the confident errors of the lawless than one merely trying to hold ground. Growth is the positive counterpart to guarding.

views.label: The command is a general closing encouragement without special weight
views.case: Some treat grow in grace as a familiar benediction-like flourish, a warm final word rather than a pointed strategy against error. On this view its force is mainly devotional. Yet its placement, directly after the warning of verse 17 and the strong therefore, ties it closely to the danger, giving the command real strategic weight in the letter's argument.

debates.bearing: Read in context, the closing command is Peter's deliberate answer to the danger of the whole letter: the best protection against false teaching and drift is ongoing growth in the grace and knowledge of Jesus Christ. The letter ends by turning defense into pursuit, and pursuit into worship of the Lord who is glorified forever.

study.originalLanguages: Peter's closing words are chosen with care. Believers are to be found aspilos, spotless, and amometos, blameless, sacrificial words for an offering without defect, in pointed contrast to the false teachers who were blots. The Lord's makrothumia, long-suffering, is to be counted salvation, the very patience the scoffers despised. Meanwhile the unstable streblousin, wrench and rack, Paul's harder words and all Scripture into ruinous shapes. So the readers must keep their sterigmos, their firm and settled footing, and above all auxanete, keep on growing, in the charis, grace, and gnosis, knowledge, of Christ. The letter that has pressed the knowledge of Christ from its opening closes by commanding its increase, and then breaks into glory offered directly to the Lord Jesus.

typology.fulfillment: The Old Testament sacrifice without defect prefigures the character in which Christ's people are to be found, itself grounded in Christ the spotless Lamb, Exodus 12:5 and 1 Peter 1:19.

typology.type: The stability of the wise builder
typology.fulfillment: The firm footing believers are to keep answers to the wisdom that builds on the rock, so that the storms of error do not carry them away, Matthew 7:24 to 25.

study.christ: The letter closes wholly on Christ. He is the Lord in whose sight His people are to be found spotless and blameless, the Savior whose patience is salvation, and the One in whose grace and knowledge believers are to grow without ceasing. The final doxology, to Him be the glory both now and to the day of eternity, is worship offered directly to Jesus, a plain confession that the Lord and Savior is God, worthy of eternal glory. The whole aim of the Christian life, and the sure defense against error, is to know and grow in Him.
study.god: God is gracious, patient, and faithful. His patience is not delay but salvation, the time in which He saves. He gave Paul wisdom to write, so that the apostolic letters stand with the rest of the Scriptures as His inspired word. His grace is the very ground in which His people grow, and His purpose is that they be found blameless at the last. The letter's closing glory to Christ reveals the Son as fully God, sharing the eternal glory that belongs to God alone.

crossReferences.note: Chosen to be holy and blameless in His sight, the goal Peter presses in verse 14.
God's patience is meant to lead to repentance, the truth behind counting His patience as salvation, verse 15.
All Scripture is God-breathed, alongside Peter's placing of Paul's letters among the Scriptures, verse 16.
Growing up in all things into Christ, the aim of the closing command to grow, verse 18.

study.oneStory: The Bible's warnings against error end not in anxiety but in growth and worship. Across the whole story God matures His people toward the Day, calling them to be found spotless and to keep increasing in the grace and knowledge of Christ. The unstable who twist the word are set against those who stand firm and grow, and the letter closes by lifting all glory to the Lord Jesus, in whom the knowledge of God is found and to whom the eternal praise of His people belongs.

sermon.title: Grow in Grace
sermon.why: From Begg's series A Study in 2 Peter, this message expounds 2 Peter 3:14-18 directly: the call to be found at peace and blameless, the Lord's patience as salvation, Paul's letters as Scripture that the unstable twist, and the closing charge to stand firm and grow in the grace and knowledge of Christ. A clear, orthodox handling of this movement.

quiz.q: In light of the coming Day, what does Peter urge believers to pursue in verse 14?

options.text: To make every effort to be found at peace, spotless and blameless in His sight.
options.note: Verse 14: hope in the new creation issues in the pursuit of purity and peace with God.

options.text: To withdraw from the world and wait passively for the end.
options.note: Peter calls for active effort toward blamelessness, not passive withdrawal.

options.text: To live in a way that anticipates and prepares for these things.
options.note: True to verse 14, though the specific aim is to be found spotless and blameless at peace.

options.text: To secure their reputation among other believers.
options.note: The concern is to be blameless in His sight, before God, not to guard human reputation.

quiz.q: How are believers to regard the Lord's patient delay, according to verse 15?

options.text: As salvation, the time in which the Lord graciously saves.
options.note: Verse 15: what scoffers mocked as slowness, believers count as saving mercy.

options.text: As proof that the promise of His coming has failed.
options.note: That is the scoffers' error; Peter reads the delay as salvation, not failure.

options.text: As something Paul also wrote about with God-given wisdom.
options.note: True to verse 15, though the point is to count the patience itself as salvation.

options.text: As a reason to delay their own repentance.
options.note: God's patience is meant to lead to repentance, not to excuse postponing it.

quiz.q: What does Peter say about Paul's letters in verse 16?

options.text: Some parts are hard to understand, and the ignorant and unstable distort them, as they do the rest of the Scriptures.
options.note: Verse 16: Peter places Paul's letters among the Scriptures and warns of their being twisted.

options.text: They are so clear that no one could ever misread them.
options.note: Peter says some parts are hard to understand and are distorted by the unstable.

options.text: They are twisted by unstable people to their own destruction.
options.note: True to verse 16, though Peter also notably ranks them with the rest of the Scriptures.

options.text: They should be read only by mature teachers, not ordinary believers.
options.note: Peter warns against distortion by the unstable, not against ordinary believers reading Paul.

quiz.q: What danger does Peter warn against in verse 17?

options.text: Being carried away by the error of the lawless and falling from a secure standing.
options.note: Verse 17: forewarned, believers must guard against being swept from their firm footing.

options.text: Becoming too rigid and unwilling to consider new teaching.
options.note: The danger is the opposite: being carried away by error, not being too firm in truth.

options.text: Losing their stability by drifting after the lawless.
options.note: True to verse 17, essentially restating the warning about a secure standing.

options.text: Neglecting to memorize the words of the prophets.
options.note: The warning concerns being led away by error, not a failure of memorization.

quiz.q: How does the letter close in verse 18?

options.text: With a command to grow in the grace and knowledge of Christ, and glory offered to Him forever.
options.note: Verse 18: ongoing growth in Christ, sealed with a doxology to the Lord Jesus.

options.text: With a final warning that most believers will fall away.
options.note: The letter ends on growth and worship, not on a prediction of mass apostasy.

options.text: With a doxology giving glory to Christ both now and to the day of eternity.
options.note: True to verse 18, though the command to grow in grace and knowledge comes first.

options.text: With instructions to appoint new elders in each church.
options.note: Peter's closing concerns growth and glory to Christ, not church appointments.

questions: Peter links the hope of the new creation to being found spotless and blameless. How does looking forward to that Day actually shape the choices you make this week?
The Lord's patience is to be counted as salvation. Who in your life might God be patiently waiting for, and how can you join His patience with prayer and witness?
Peter warns that the unstable twist Scripture to their own ruin. What habits help you read the Bible humbly and rightly rather than bending it to what you already want?
Believers are warned not to be carried away and lose their firm footing. What tends to pull you off balance, and where do you most need to be grounded in the truth?
The letter ends with a command to grow, not merely to hold on. What would growing in the grace and knowledge of Christ look like for you in this season?

translationNotes.note: Spotless and blameless render aspilos and amometos, sacrificial terms for an offering without defect; a footnote allows to be found by Him in peace, without spot and without blemish.
Patience renders makrothymia, long-suffering; our beloved brother Paul shows the warm regard between the apostles and the early circulation of Paul's letters.
Distort renders strebloo, to twist or wrench; the rest of the Scriptures places Paul's letters among the inspired writings, an early witness to the New Testament canon.
Grow renders auxano, a present continuous call to ongoing increase; the closing doxology offers glory directly to Christ. Some manuscripts do not include the final Amen.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched from bible.helloao.org for 2 Peter 3:14-18. All verses confirmed against the API. In verse 14 the BSB em dash before spotless has been replaced with a comma to comply with the no-dash rule; wording is otherwise unchanged.
